coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 heterojunction photoanode for enhancing photoelectrochemical water splitting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Shiyu
Liu, Ziyang
Gao, Yuanpan
Zhang, Ruoran
Zhu, Rui
Gao, Shanshan
Guan, Peipei
Geng, Xuanran
Tian, Ke
Bai, Shouli
coredata.dc:description: The design of photoanode materials to achieve efficient water splitting is a current research hotspot in photoelectrochemical (PEC). In this work, we designed and prepared an α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 heterostructure modified by carbon quantum dots ((α-Fe2O3/BiVO4/CQDs)) as the photoanode. The modification of CQDs can broaden the sunlight absorption ability and dramatically enhance the water oxidation kinetics of α-Fe2O3/BiVO4/CQDs. The optimized photoanode demonstrated a photocurrent density of 1.32 mA·cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is 2.2 times than pristine α-Fe2O3 (0.6 mA·cm−2) photoanode. The IPCE value above 29.4 % at 400 nm, and the onset potential is negatively shifted by 243 mV for α-Fe2O3/BiVO4/CQDs. The improved performance is mainly due to the α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 heterojunction to achieve effective separation of photogenerated carriers in the bulk phase and CQDs to improve light utilisation and OER kinetics of the photoanode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEC
Heterojunction
α-Fe2O3
BiVO4
Carbon quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of the α-Fe2O3 with (a) NaF, (b) NaNO3 and (c) Na2SO4 as additives. (d) XRD patterns of the α-Fe2O3 films with different additives.
(a, b) SEM images of BiOI; Top-view and side-view SEM images of (c, d) BiVO4, (e, f) FBV(150 s) and (g, h) FBV/CQDs-2.
(a) EDX spectrum and (b) Elemental mappings of FBV/CQDs-2.
(a) TEM image of carbon quantum dots. (b) PL spectra of synthesized CQDs at 300, 350, 400, 450 and 500 nm. (c) XRD patterns of α-Fe2O3, BiVO4, FBV(150 s), and FBV/CQDs-2.
UV–vis absorbtion spectra of α-Fe2O3, BiVO4, FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2.
XPS spectra of (a) survey scan; (b) Fe 2p; (c) Bi 4f; (d) V 2p; (e) O 1 s and (f) C 1 s of FBV/CQDs-2.

Photocurrent–potential curves of (a) different α-Fe2O3 photoanodes; (b) BiVO4, α-Fe2O3, FBV(120 s), FBV(150 s) and FBV(180 s) photoanodes; (c) FBV/CQDs-1, FBV/CQDs-2 and FBV/CQDs-3 photoanodes. (d) Chopped light irradiation of α-Fe2O3, FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2 photoanodes.
(a) Nyquist plots, (b) Bode and (c) IPCE plots of the α-Fe2O3, BiVO4, FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2 photoanodes. (d) comparison of the onset potentials and photocurrent densities at different potentials.
(a) M–S and Tauc plots of (b) α-Fe2O3 and (c) BiVO4.
VB-XPS spectra of (a) Fe2O3 and (b) BiVO4.
Schematic diagram of the proposed charge transfer mechanism of ternary FBV/CQDs-2 heterojunction.
References related to PEC performance of Fe2O3/BiVO4 and Fe2O3/CQDs based photoanodes.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 heterojunction photoanode for enhancing photoelectrochemical water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aInstitute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Environmentally Harmful Chemicals Analysis, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Environmentally Harmful Chemicals Analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bState Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Environmentally Harmful Chemicals Analysis, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSchool of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of photoanode materials to achieve efficient water splitting is a current research hotspot in photoelectrochemical (PEC). In this work, we designed and prepared an α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 heterostructure modified by carbon quantum dots ((α-Fe2O3/BiVO4/CQDs)) as the photoanode. The modification of CQDs can broaden the sunlight absorption ability and dramatically enhance the water oxidation kinetics of α-Fe2O3/BiVO4/CQDs. The optimized photoanode demonstrated a photocurrent density of 1.32 mA·cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is 2.2 times than pristine α-Fe2O3 (0.6 mA·cm−2) photoanode. The IPCE value above 29.4 % at 400 nm, and the onset potential is negatively shifted by 243 mV for α-Fe2O3/BiVO4/CQDs. The improved performance is mainly due to the α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 heterojunction to achieve effective separation of photogenerated carriers in the bulk phase and CQDs to improve light utilisation and OER kinetics of the photoanode.
The ternary photoanode of CQDs modified Fe2O3/BiVO4 was successfully fabricated, which achieve efficient PEC water splitting performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The conversion of water into hydrogen by means of photoelectrochemistry (PEC) is considered to be an effective technology for solving the energy crisis [1–3]. In the recent years, more and more semiconductor materials have been actively developed by researchers in the related fields since TiO2 was discovered to be able to carry out photo-induced driven water splitting process [4,5]. Metal oxide semiconductors such as Fe2O3, SnO2, MoO3 are among the more widely studied classes of photoanode materials [6–8]. In addition, there are some interesting materials such as metal sulfides (ZnS, ZnIn2S4), organic semiconductors (g-C3N4, polymer polydopamine) and so on, while the in-depth development of the materials is of great significance for future technology paths, but these materials are basically non-green, poor stability and uncontrollable cost. Hematite is a promising cost-controllable and efficient photoanode material with energy band positions matched to the water oxidation reaction, which can theoretically achieve a maximum solar energy conversion efficiency of 15.5 % [9,10]. Currently, many researchers have also been progressively optimising hematite so that it can be expected to achieve the desired PEC performance [11,12]. For a single hematite, it is widely accepted that the limitations of its performance in the PEC field are mainly limited by the short hole diffusion lengths, poor conductivity, high electron-hole pair recombination rates, slow water oxidation kinetics [13,14]. Therefore, the optimal design of photoanodes is crucial, and the redesign of hematite by morphology modulation, energy band modulation or surface modification is currently a hot topic in PEC [15,16]. By combining one or several modification methods, semiconductor materials can be made to have unexpected performance enhancements [17–19].

Bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is currently regarded as an n-type semiconductor of great research value, its monoclinic-scheelite phase has relatively good photovoltaic properties, [20–22] and it is generally accepted that it has excellent performance as a water splitting anode with stable chemical properties [23]. In addition, it has energy band positions with α-Fe2O3 that can form a staggered structure, which provides the possibility of constructing type II heterostructure of the α-Fe2O3 and BiVO4 to enhance optical and electrical properties. Xia et al. reported a BiVO4 photoanode catalyzed with an ultrathin α-Fe2O3 layer that had excellent heterojunction effects for Suppressing the recombination between carriers [24]. Li et al. co-doped Mo, W into BiVO4 and constructed heterojunction with α-Fe2O3 exhibited high PEC performance [25]. However, heterojunctions can saturate the built-in electric field due to charge accumulation, which limits their contribution to the water oxidation process [26]. Therefore, many researchers have compounded suitable catalysts with heterojunctions to improve the OER performance of photoelectrodes, such as oxyhydroxides (FeOOH, CoOOH) [27], CoPi[28] and Various cationic compositions of LDHs [29]. Shao et al. acoustochemically loaded structurally disordered FeOOH onto CdIn2S4 and tuned its phase structure, which resulted in more active sites at CdIn2S4/FeOOH photoanode to participate in the OER reaction process and allowed for the prolongation of the photogenerated carrier lifetime [30]. Chen et al. modified AgAu nanoclusters on the surface of BiVO4 to improve its charge transport and photosensitization properties, and used CoNi-LDH as a hole-collecting layer and co-catalyst to improve the OER kinetics of the ternary photoelectrode, the structural design of CoNi-LDH/AgAu NCs/BiVO4 photoanode obtained excellent PEC performance [31]. Apart from the rational design of the heterogeneous structure, the modification of the co-catalysts also facilitates the water splitting process of the photoanode [32]. Quantum dots (QDs) is a relatively novel material in the field of photoelectrochemistry, with its own quantum confinement effect that can be adjusted in size to achieve desired optical properties. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is considered to be excellent catalytic materials due to its great chemical stability, environmental friendliness and high carrier mobility [33,34]. Furthermore, the conduction band position of BiVO4 is lower than the LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) energy level of CQDs, which means that under light excitation, the BiVO4 will tend to receive electrons from the LUMO of CQDs during the PEC process. Zhou et al. reported a CQDs modified TiO2 photoanode, the photocurrent density (1.23 V vs. RHE) of TiO2/CQDs can be nearly three times that of pristine TiO2 [35].

In this work, we designed an α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 heterostructure through energy band engineering that significantly reduces the recombination probability between photocarriers. Afterwards, the heterojunction was modified by CQDs to improve the OER dynamics of the photoanode and enhance its light-harvesting capability. Finally, we explored the potential working mechanism of α-Fe2O3/BiVO4/CQDs photoanode. The results show that the heterostructure and CQDs not only play specific roles, but the synergistic mechanism of the two also works excellently to make the photoanode exhibit desirable photoelectrochemical performance.

2.25 mmol FeCl3·6H2O (Macklin, 99 %) was dissolved in 15 mL deionized (DI) water with different additives. Specifically, in order to obtain different morphologies of α-Fe2O3, 4.5 mmol NaF (Macklin, 99 %), 5.5 mmol NaNO3 (Sigma-Aldrich, 99 %) and Na2SO4 (Mreda, 99.95 %) were added to the hydrothermal reaction solution as additives. Afterwards, three photoanodes were hydrothermally treated for 10 h at 95 °C to obtain FeOOH. The precursor was calcined at 600 °C for 2 h and further 700 °C for 15 min (5 °C/min) for conversion to α-Fe2O3. The three photoanode are denoted as α-Fe2O3
NaF, α-Fe2O3
NaNO3 and α-Fe2O3
Na2SO4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BiVO4 and α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 photoanode
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 0.679 g Bi(NO3)3 ⋅5H2O (Acmes, 99.9 %) and 2.324 g KI (Macklin, 99 %) were added 35 mL DI water. The pH was adjusted to 1.7 by HNO3 and subsequently another solution was prepared by adding 0.348 g p-benzoquinone (Macklin, 99 %) to 14 mL anhydrous ethanol. Later on, the two solutions were mixed in order to obtain the electrolyte. A typical three-electrode system (Ag/AgCl, Pt counter electrode, α-Fe2O3/FTO working electrode) was used to deposit BiOI on the surface of α-Fe2O3 at −0.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl for 150 s. After deposition, the α-Fe2O3/BiOI was slowly flushed with deionized water. Then 0.053 g VO(acac)2 was dissolved in 1 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide solution (DMSO) (Macklin, 99.9 %). 50 μL of VO(acac)2 (Aladdin, 98 %) precursor solutions was applied to the α-Fe2O3/BiOI surface and then annealed at 450 °C for 2 h (2 °C/min). Afterwards, the photoanode immersed in 1 mol/L NaOH to dislodge V2O5. To get the optimal condition, three α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 films with different deposition times (120 s, 150 s and 180 s) were prepared, which are denoted as FBV(120 s ), FBV(150 s) and FBV(180 s), respectively. In order to prepare BiVO4 photoanode, it can be deposited directly on the FTO without α-Fe2O3 film during the electrodeposition process.

The CQDs were synthesized through hydrothermal method. 2 g citric acid (Macklin, 99.5 %) was dissolved in 20 mL N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, Aladdin, 98 %). α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 was hydrothermally reacted in the solution at 160 °C for 6 h After cooled, 40 mL 50 mg/mL NaOH (Macklin, 96 %) was added into the solution and the CQD was obtained by centrifugation after being mixed homogeneously. Pipette 100 μL CQDs solution and transfer it to the surface of α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 film. Then spun-coated at 2000 rpm for 30 s. Subsequently, it was kept under nitrogen atmosphere at 200 °C for 30 min to enhance the stability of the material structure. For comparison, three α-Fe2O3/BiVO4/CQDs films with different speed (1000, 2000 and 3000 rpm) of spin-coating were prepared, which are denoted as FBV/CQDs-1, FBV/CQDs-2 and FBV/CQDs-3, respectively.
Fig. 1 shows the fabrication process of photoanodes. And the sample photographs of BiVO4, α-Fe2O3, FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2 are shown in Fig. S1.

The instrument models used in the characterization tests are indicated in the Supplyment (SI). Fig. 2 (a) shows the SEM image of α-Fe2O3
NaF, which consists of irregular nanoparticles. The diameters of these nanoparticles are from 50 to 100 nm. The SEM of α-Fe2O3
NaNO3 as shown in Fig. 2(b), the nanoparticles on the film are spindle-shaped, it showed irregular distribution on the FTO with width and length of 40–60 and ∼100 nm. Fig. 2(c) shows the SEM image of α-Fe2O3
Na2SO4, which is clearly observed that α-Fe2O3
Na2SO4 was bonded by many polyhedral blocks. With further detailed observation, these polyhedra are made up of a combination of many nanoparticles with sizes in the range of 10–30 nm. Fig. 2(d) shows the XRD of three Fe2O3 with different additives, the characteristic peaks observed of above samples correspond to α-Fe2O3 (JCPDS#33–0664) [36]. And there are no miscellaneous peaks in the plots except for the characteristic peaks attributed to FTO that can be observed. In addition, it can be seen that the (104) crystal plane of α-Fe2O3 with Na2SO4 and NaNO3 as the additives is higher than (110), which may be attributed to the SO4 2− and NO3 − the sample to grow preferentially at the (104) crystal plane, leading to higher diffraction intensity. In contrast, F − is a strong coordination anion that may form coordination interactions with Fe3+, leading to changes in the growth kinetics and thermodynamic stability of the crystals. It may inhibit the preferential growth of (104) crystal plane, leading to the relatively easy formation of (110).

To make a preliminary determination of the structural composition of the ternary material, BiOI, BiVO4, FBV(150 s), and FBV/CQDs-2 photoanode were characterized by the scanning electron microscope (SEM). Fig. 3 (a, b) exhibits a sheet-like morphology of BiOI, which the lengh is about 900–1000 nm with a smooth surface coated on FTO uniformly. After calcination, the sheet structure transformed into irregularly shaped but uniformly distributed nanoparticles. The thickness of BiVO4 was 880 nm (Fig. 3(d)). Fig. 3(e) shows the SEM of FBV via electrodeposition-MOD method. The morphology of BiVO4 is substantially dominated by the structure of BiOI, as can be seen in the side view SEM (Fig. 3(f)) where the semiconductors are closely connected in the FBV(150 s) with a thickness of 1120 nm, and single component synthesized by the corresponding method can be observed from Fig. 3(d) and Fig. S2 to be about 900 (BiVO4) and 200 nm (α-Fe2O3). In Fig. 3(g), the modification of CQDs did not alter the morphology of FBV/CQDs-2, and from Fig. 3(h) it can be observed that the thickness of FBV/CQDs-2 is approximate 1130 nm, which is almost uniform with FBV (150 s).

To confirm the composition of FBV/CQDs-2, the EDX and elemental mappings have been carried out (Fig. 4 (a)). The results indicate the uniform distribution of C element on the surface of FBV/CQDs-2 with the atomic ratio of 18.03 At%. Namely, Bi, V, O, and Fe and their elemental proportions in the composite are 7.4, 7.55, 60.92 and 6.09 At%, respectively. The EDX spectrum reveals and confirms that the α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 and CQDs are effectively incorporated to obtain the composite. Fig. 2(b) shows the element mapping C, Bi, V, O and Fe, which indicates all the element are evenly distributed on the FBV/CQDs-2 surface.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image of CQDs with the size distribution from 2 to 3 nm with good liquid-phase dispersibility. Thereafter, this work adjusted different excitation wavelengths (λex) of 300, 350, 400, 450 and 500 nm (Fig. 5(b)) for photoluminescence (PL) testing to probe the optical properties of the material. It can be seen that the PL intensity changes at different wavelengths, and its maximum emission intensity at 450 nm excitation. Surface state and size effects affecting the CQDs band gap are believed to be responsible for the complexity of the PL behaviour [37,38].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was utilized to study the components and the crystalline phase of α-Fe2O3, BiVO4, FBV(150 s), and FBV/CQDs-2 (Fig. 5(c)). The peaks related to BiVO4 were obtained at 2θ = 18.67, 18.99, 28.59, 28.82, 28.95, 30.55, 34.49, 35.22°, corresponding to (110), (011), (−130), (−121), (121), (040), (200) and (002) planes, which matches well with JCPDS#14–0688, confirming evolution of the monoclinic phase BiVO4 [39]. The diffraction peaks for α-Fe2O3 match well with JCPDS#33–0664. The peaks are present at 2θ = 24.14, 33.15, 35.61°, corresponding to (012), (104), (110), thus confirm the formation of the rhombohedral hematite phase [40]. The peaks at 26.61, 37.95, 51.78, 61.87 and 65.94° correspond to FTO [41]. For FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2, the characteristic peaks of α-Fe2O3, BiVO4 and substrate are all detected without any remaining stray peaks, except that the distinct diffraction peaks are not detected due to the trace loads of CQDs.

The light absorption properties of α-Fe2O3, BiVO4, FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2 were measured over the wavelength range of 300−800 nm by UV–vis absorption spectra. From Fig. 6 , all of the as-prepared photoanodes possess high light absorption ability from UV light region to visible. After growth of BiVO4 film on α-Fe2O3 film, the resulting FBV(150 s) displays a significant red shift, indicating the α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 heterojunction optimizes the absorption of sunlight, compared with α-Fe2O3 and BiVO4, it is clear that a larger redshift occurs in FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2, and due to the unique optical properties of CQDs, the FBV/CQDs-2 photoanode can utilize light more efficiently than FBV(150 s).

The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of FBV/CQDs-2 were investigated in Fig. 7 . The survey scan indicates the existence of all elements of Fe, Bi, V, O and C in FBV/CQDs-2 composite (Fig. 7(a)). The peaks at 710.2 and 724.2 eV in FBV/CQDs-2 corresponded to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, which is the characteristic of the Fe3+ present in FBV/CQDs-2. A characteristic satellite peak corresponding to α-Fe2O3 also appeared at 712.3 and 719.3 eV [42]. In addition, from the spectra of Fe 2p of different photoanodes in Fig. S3, it can be observed that after the formation of the heterostructure, the peaks of Fe 2p undergoes a shift toward lower binding energy. This indicates that the transfer of electrons occurred during the formation of the heterostructure and that the electrons flowed from BiVO4 to Fe2O3, which is consistent with the direction of electron transfer after the actual construction of the heterojunction, further confirming the successful formation of heterojunction. As can be seen in the O 1 s of the three photoanodes in Fig. S4, for single α-Fe2O3, only two characteristic peaks can be classified, which are attributed to chemical lattice oxygen OL at 530.0 eV and hydroxyl oxygen OOH at 530.8 eV, respectively. However, after heterojunction formation and modification of CQDs, the characteristic peaks of oxygen vacancies Ov can be observed at 530.7 eV The peaks at 158.2 and 163.5 eV were assigned to Bi3+ 4f7/2 and 4f5/2. The peaks centered at 515.7 eV assigned to V 2p3/2, 523.2 eV assigned to V 2p1/2, this demonstrates the presence of V5+ species [43]. Fig. 7(e) and Fig. S4 illustrates the O 1 s spectral region of ternary and three different photoanodes. As can be seen in the O 1 s of the different photoanodes in Fig. S4, for single α-Fe2O3, only two characteristic peaks can be classified, which are attributed to chemical lattice oxygen OL at 530.0 eV and hydroxyl oxygen OOH at 530.8 eV, respectively. However, after heterojunction formation and modification of CQDs, the characteristic peaks of oxygen vacancies Ov can be observed at 530.7 eV, OL and OOH then move to 529.8 eV and 531.8 eV respectively. Fig. 7(f) shows the C 1 s spectra, the peaks at 284.5, 285.7, and 287.5 eV are corresponding to CC, CO and C = O in the CQDs [44]. In conclusion, these results demonstrate that the FBV/CQDs-2 was indeed successfully synthesized.

The instrument models and test conditions used in the PEC measurements section are indicated in the SI. The photocurrent density discussed in this section are, unless otherwise noted, at 1.23 V vs. RHE. In general, the optimal electrolyte for α-Fe2O3 photoanode is a strong alkaline solution of 1 M NaOH (pH = 13.6). The voltage values discussed in this section are all vs. RHE. The PEC performances of three photoanodes have been investigated in Fig. 8 (a), the curves show that all α-Fe2O3 photoanodes exhibit photoresponse under light conditions. The water splitting process starts to occur when the applied external voltage is close to ∼0.8 V vs. RHE, and the photocurrent densities of several photoanodes are almost negligible under the dark environment. The test results showed that the photo-anodes prepared with NaF as additive could reach a photocurrent density of ∼0.6 mA·cm−2, while the photocurrent densities of α-Fe2O3 photo-anodes obtained with NaNO3 and Na2SO4 as additives were ∼0.07 and ∼0.04 mA·cm−2, respectively. Combined with the SEM image analysis, this result may be attributed to the smaller size of the nanoparticles in the α-Fe2O3
NaF photoanode, which has a higher specific surface area and can provide more reactive active sites for the water splitting. In order to exclude the effect of BiVO4 in FBV(150 s) to verify that the performance enhancement of FBV(150 s) is attributed to the optimization of heterogeneous structure rather than to the single BiVO4, so the data of BiVO4 were introduced for comparison, which showed a photocurrent density of 0.7 mA·cm−2. To investigate the effects of the BiVO4 layer, we fabricated α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 films with different deposit durations. In order to obtain the different BiVO4 upper layer, BiOI deposition duration was set to 120 s, 150 s and 180 s. The J-V curves of the α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 photoanode were obtained for varying deposition durations of BiOI. The heterojunction photoanode photocurrent was gradually increased by applying an external voltage for 120 and 150 s of deposition. The FBV(150 s) showed the maximum photocurrent density of 1.1 mA·cm−2, 1.57 times and 1.83 times higher than BiVO4 and α-Fe2O3. However, when the deposition time was extended to 180 s, its water splitting performance underwent a substantial weakening, which may be attributed to the fact that BiVO4 with appropriate thickness can construct an efficient heterostructure with α-Fe2O3 and promote the separation of photogenerated carriers, but when the layer of BiVO4 is too thick, the resistance to electron transfer between α-Fe2O3 and the electrode surface for carriers gradually increases, which causes further deterioration in the electron transfer characteristics.

In ternary photoanodes, different speed (1000, 2000 and 3000 rpm) of spin-coating determined the amount of CQDs. Fig. 8(c) shows the photocurrent response with various amounts of CQDs. The photocurrent density of FBV/CQDs-2 photoanode has the highest value (1.32 mA·cm−2), which is 2.2 times for α-Fe2O3 photoanode. This may be due to the generation of electron/hole pairs in the LUMO and HOMO energy levels, respectively. After photoexcitation of CQDs, where electrons in the LUMO energy level tend to be transferred to the conduction band of BiVO4 due to the presence of the electric potential, and a large number of photogenerated holes can be retained in HOMO and participate in water oxidation reactions. Fig. 8(d) shows the photocurrent density tests under switching between light and dark environments for α-Fe2O3, FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2 under chopped illumination at a frequency bar of 2 s. The experimental results show that all the above photoanodes exhibit rapid photoresponse to visible light and confirm that the process of water oxidation occurs mainly under light conditions. Table 1 listed the Fe2O3/BiVO4 and Fe2O3/CQDs based photoanodes and their PEC properties for water splitting.

In order to probe the interfacial charge transfer kinetics, we performed electrochemical impedance spectroscopies (EIS) tests on BiVO4, α-Fe2O3, FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2 photoanodes as shown in Fig. 9 (a). The larger radius of the semicircular arc can be understood as the larger interfacial resistance between electrode and electrolyte, and the slow charge transfer kinetics of hematite photoanode mainly attributed to energy mismatch between its acceptor d orbitals of hematite and donor p orbitals of the O2
OH- redox couple and surface states . The FBV/CQDs-2 has the smallest Rct (481 Ω), compared with BiVO4 (1009 Ω), α-Fe2O3 (1357 Ω) and FBV(150 s) (843 Ω), indicating the fastest charge transfer kinetics at the FBV/CQDs-2/electrolyte interface. It is observed that the semicircle diameter in the plot was reduced with the introduction of BiVO4 and CQDs, which indicated the structural design of FBV/CQDs is more favorable for the carrier transport process. The FBV/CQDs-2 photoanode with lower resistance Rct indicates the dramatically improved charge transfer kinetics attributed to the construction of α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 heterojunction and decoration of CQDs. For FBV/CQDs-2 photoanode, CQDs can facilitate carrier transport by acting as a carrier for charge transfer between the heterojunction and the electrolyte, achieving efficient electron-hole pair separation and improving the water oxidation kinetics of the photoanode [49,50].

From the Bode plots in Fig. 9(b), BiVO4, α-Fe2O3 and FBV(150 s) have one peak while FBV/CQDs-2 has two peaks, indicating that BiVO4, α-Fe2O3 and FBV(150 s) have one time constant, and FBV/CQDs-2 has two time constants. Bode plots reflect the efficient electron lifetime in BiVO4, α-Fe2O3, FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2 photoanodes. The lifetime (τe) can be determined by Eq. (S1) [51]. The fmax value for BiVO4, α-Fe2O3, FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2 is 125.9, 208.8, 74.1 and 25.1 Hz, which suggests that FBV/CQDs-2 possesses 8.3-fold enhanced lifetime of electrons than α-Fe2O3. It benefits from in FBV/CQDs-2 structure, the charge-carrier complexation at the photoanode is suppressed due to the synergistic mechanism of FBV heterostructure and CQDs, resulting in an improved charge separation efficiency.

To further evaluate FBV/CQDs-2 structure, the incident photon-to current efficiency (IPCE) was also obtained under PEC water oxidation. This constructed FBV/CQDs-2 photoanode was optimized to exhibit high IPCE values in the wavelength range of 380 to 600 nm compared to BiVO4 (Fig. 9(c)), which is consistent with the LSV plots. The IPCEs at 400 nm are 15.3, 18, 24.5, and 29.4 % for α-Fe2O3, BiVO4, FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2. The onset potential was defined as the potential at the intersection point of the tangent at a maximum slope of photocurrent and the dark current curve. As shown in Fig. 9(d), the onset potential of FBV(150 s) (694 mV) exhibits a prominent cathodic shift of 185 mV and 49 mV compared to α-Fe2O3 (879 mV) and BiVO4 (739 mV), respectively. Compared to the FBV(150 s) photoanode, the onset potential of FBV/CQDs-2 photoanode (636 mV) has a significant cathodic shift of 58 mV, it suggests that the OER kinetics is enhanced upon the deposition of CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 10 (a), the flat band potentials (VFB) were estimated by the Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots. The α-Fe2O3, BiVO4, FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2 photoanodes all show positive slope of the M-S curve, suggesting the n-type semiconductor for all photoanodes and the semiconductor type of α-Fe2O3 is unaffected by BiVO4 and CQDs modification. The VFB can be calculated according to the Eq. (S2) and (S3) [39]. Obtained by the X-axis intercept, the VFB of BiVO4, α-Fe2O3, FBV(150 s) are 0.24, 0.65 and 0.58 V vs. RHE. After modification of CQDs, the VFB is negatively shifted to 0.51 V vs. RHE. It suggests increased band bending of the FBV/CQDs-2, which is beneficial for facilitating the promotion of carrier transport in electrode/electrolyte interface. In addition, VFB can also be used to estimate the conduction band positions of Fe2O3 and BiVO4 with values 0.1–0.3 eV above the VFB position. Assuming potential difference between the CB and VFB is 0.2 eV, the CB potentials of BiVO4 and α-Fe2O3 are 0.04 eV and 0.45 eV vs. RHE. Accordingly, the VB of α-Fe2O3 and BiVO4 are 2.53 and 2.44 eV vs. RHE. The calculated carrier densities of α-Fe2O3, BiVO4, FBV(150 s) and FBV/CQDs-2 photoanodes are 7.38 × 1019, 8.59 × 1019, 5.22 × 1020 and 8.32 × 1020 cm−3. The bandgap energies were calculated using the Eq. (S4). The absorption edges of α-Fe2O3, BiVO4 were 595 and 515 nm, which correspond to the bandgap energy of 2.08 and 2.4 eV (Fig. 10(b, c)).

Furthermore, although the VB values of Fe2O3 and BiVO4 can be empirically inferred from the Mott-Schottky. However, to ensure the accuracy of the experimental results, we obtained two semiconductors by performing the XPS-VB test as shown in Fig. 11 . The calculation formula using the Eq. (S5). According to the calculations and combined with UV–vis, the CB values of Fe2O3 and BiVO4 are 2.59 eV and 2.52 eV, and the positions of the valence bands are 0.51 eV and 0.12 eV It is confirmed that the above two materials possess the conditions to constitute a type II heterojunction.

Fig. 12 presents the PEC mechanism of FBV/CQDs-2 photoanode. When the heterojunction was exposed to sunlight, the electrons were excitated from α-Fe2O3 and BiVO4 valence bands to their conduction bands, respectively. Since both the CB and VB positions of BiVO4 are more negative than α-Fe2O3, the electrons excited to the CB in BiVO4 will flow to the CB of α-Fe2O3, and the holes in the VB will flow from α-Fe2O3 to BiVO4 in the reverse direction [52,53]. For the FBV/CQDs-2, the LUMO and HOMO energy levels of CQDs output electrons and receive holes into BiVO4 due to the distinct energy band positions, and the holes accumulated in HOMO favor the oxidation reaction [35,48,54]. After modifying the CQDs, the FBV/CQDs-2 photoanode have an extended visible light response range, partly due to the introduction of more hydroxyl groups on their surfaces after sunlight irradiation, creating more surface defects and thus improving the light absorption efficiency, and partly stemming from the fact that CQDs can improve the visible light collection at the FBV (150 s) through the up-conversion effect generated by the absorption of two or more photons [55,56].

In summary, the design of FBV/CQDs-2 photoanode based on the energy-band theory successfully optimizes the role of carriers for the OER process, and intensively explored the optimal loading of BiVO4 and CQDs on α-Fe2O3. The photocurrent density and IPCE value of FBV/CQDs-2 photoanode were 2.2 and 1.92 times higher than bare α-Fe2O3 photoanode. The enhanced performance of FBV(150 s) photoanodes could be attributed to the formation of α-Fe2O3/BiVO4 n-n heterojunction with efficient charge separation. The light absorption range is further extended and the interfacial charge transfer is accelerated by the additional decorating of CQDs. This kind of FBV/CQDs-2 heterojunction photoelectrode with good PEC performance could have potential application in the field of energy conversion.

Shiyu Jia: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Ziyang Liu: Resources. Yuanpan Gao: Formal analysis. Ruoran Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Rui Zhu: Software. Shanshan Gao: Software. Peipei Guan: Writing – original draft. Xuanran Geng: Software. Ke Tian: Writing – original draft, Supervision. Shouli Bai: Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any conflict of interest in the work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51772015) and Beijing Key Laboratory of Environmentally Harmful Chemicals Analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2024.145621.
